one_line_takeaway: SHIELDAGENT turns natural-language safety policies into learned, action-scoped probabilistic LTL rule circuits so a guardrail agent can formally verify another web agent's actions, beating prior LLM guardrails in accuracy, recall, and false-positive rate while sharply cutting API calls and latency.
key_finding: On SHIELDAGENT-BENCH, SHIELDAGENT reaches overall guardrail accuracy of 90.4% (agent-based) and 91.7% (environment-based), the lowest false-positive rate (5.6%/4.0%, 4.8% overall) and highest average rule recall (87.5%/92.7%, 90.1% overall), outperforming the best baseline (Rule Traverse) by ~10.2% average accuracy while cutting closed-source API queries by 64.7% and inference time by 58.2%. On three existing benchmarks it leads by 7.4% average accuracy (ST-Web 91.1%, VWA-Adv 94.1%, AgentHarm 86.9%). In the online setting it yields the highest policy-compliance rate in all six web environments (e.g., GitLab 22.8% base AWM -> 50.7% with SHIELDAGENT). The abstract's headline gain is stated as 11.3% (vs. 10.2% in the results section).
core_contribution: SHIELDAGENT is presented as the first LLM-based guardrail agent that shields the action trajectories of OTHER LLM-based autonomous (web) agents by enforcing explicit safety-policy compliance through probabilistic logical reasoning, rather than text-based content moderation. Its own two-part contribution is: (1) an Action-based Safety Policy Model (ASPM) that automatically extracts verifiable linear-temporal-logic (LTL) rules from policy documents (e.g. EU AI Act, GitLab handbook), iteratively refines/prunes them, and organizes them into action-based probabilistic rule circuits whose weights are learned; and (2) a verification framework in which, given an invoked agent action, only the relevant rule circuit is retrieved, a shielding plan is generated from a tool library and hybrid memory, formal-verification code assigns predicate truth values, and probabilistic (Markov Logic Network) inference returns a binary safety label, the list of violated rules, and a textual explanation. The paper also contributes SHIELDAGENT-BENCH, a new agent-guardrail benchmark. Note: prior guardrails GuardAgent, LlamaGuard, LlavaGuard, SafeWatch, r2-guard are cited as related/baseline work, not as this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Malicious or adversarially manipulated LLM web agents whose action trajectories may violate safety policies, causing privacy breaches, financial loss, or unsafe operations. The paper explicitly considers two adversarial perturbation sources: (1) agent-based attacks that manipulate internal agent components (instructions, memory/knowledge base, tools) — instantiated by AgentPoison, which poisons the agent's memory/knowledge base; and (2) environment-based attacks that manipulate the environment the agent perceives (malicious HTML elements, deceptive pop-ups) — instantiated by AdvWeb, which stealthily perturbs web elements. SHIELDAGENT is a post-hoc external verifier ('System 2') copiloting the protected agent; it does not assume access to modify the protected agent and aims to be agnostic to attack modality by grounding decisions in externally verifiable evidence against explicit policies.
limitations: The paper's own results show weaker performance on hallucination-related risks (85.5%/86.7% accuracy), which it attributes to needing external knowledge beyond the policy. The whole pipeline depends on GPT-4o for policy/rule extraction and refinement, plus optional human expert correction of the ASPM, so guardrail quality is bounded by that extraction quality and the availability of written policy documents. Adversarial coverage is limited to two attack algorithms (AgentPoison, AdvWeb) on a single protected agent (AWM); generalization to other agents/attacks is asserted rather than exhaustively shown. There is internal inconsistency in the benchmark's reported scale (abstract says 3K then 2K 'pairs'; body reports 960 instructions and 3110 unsafe trajectories; Table 1 lists 3110 samples / 1080 rules). No adaptive attacker that targets the guardrail itself is evaluated, and the relative-safety threshold epsilon requires manual tuning per safety-criticality level.

datasets_used: SHIELDAGENT-BENCH (own; 960 safety-related instructions, 3110 unsafe trajectories, 6 web environments: Shopping/CMS/Reddit/GitLab/Maps/SuiteCRM, 7 risk categories, 1080 rules)
VWA-Adv (200 adversarial tasks in VisualWebArena; Classifieds/Reddit/Shopping)
WebArena / ST-WebAgentBench instruction templates reused for curation
Policy sources: EU AI Act, GitLab handbook
quantitative_results: SHIELDAGENT-BENCH avg rule recall ARR@R: 87.5% (agent-based), 92.7% (environment-based); 90.1% overall
Outperforms best baseline (Rule Traverse) by ~10.2% avg accuracy on SHIELDAGENT-BENCH (abstract headline 11.3%)
Efficiency: -64.7% closed-source API queries, -58.2% inference time; NoQ 9.5/11.2 and Time 31.1s/33.8s vs Rule Traverse NoQ 27.1/31.5, Time 75.3s/80.1s
Existing benchmarks ACC: ST-Web 91.1% (FPR 4.4%), VWA-Adv 94.1% (FPR 3.4%), AgentHarm 86.9% (FPR 3.9%); +7.4% avg accuracy over baselines
Online policy-compliance rate (AWM+SHIELDAGENT): Shopping 65.3, CMS 68.4, Reddit 60.2, GitLab 50.7, Maps 80.5, SuiteCRM 55.9 (vs base AWM 46.8/53.2/45.9/22.8/67.9/36.0)
baselines_compared: Direct prompt (GPT-4o given full policy, outputs safety label + violated rules)
Rule Traverse (GPT-4o prompted per-rule; flag unsafe if any rule violated)
